key_departures.impact: Released by Washington pre-free-agency. Started 31 games over two seasons and 16 games in 2025 as the team's starting center. The Chargers quickly signed him to a 3-year, $30M deal. Leaves a major void at center with only Nick Allegretti (re-signed to 2yr deal) as the primary internal option.

Released by Washington, saving $18.5M in cap space with zero dead money. Was limited by injuries (ACL tear in November 2025). The Commanders entered free agency with only Trey Amos and Mike Sainristil as guaranteed CB roster players. Departure escalates a pre-existing CB need.

Signed a 2-year, $11M deal with the Titans. Posted a career-high 5.5 sacks in 2025 and was Washington's most reliable edge rusher when Dorance Armstrong tore his ACL. His departure would have been concerning absent Washington's aggressive EDGE spending.
Signed a 1-year deal with the division-rival Eagles. Started 7 of 12 games for Washington in 2025. His departure further thins an already shallow CB room.

Not tendered as an RFA; signed a 2-year, $12M deal with Jacksonville. Part of a thin running back room that leaves Jacory Croskey-Merritt as essentially the only RB under contract before new additions.

Contract voided at the start of the new league year, triggering a $12.4M dead cap hit. Was acquired from the 49ers in 2025 for a 5th-round pick but underperformed expectations. His departure removes a starting-caliber WR from the roster and escalates the already-critical WR need.
A pending UFA coming off a torn ACL. Washington opted not to re-sign him, instead replacing him with Chig Okonkwo. Ertz was not a long-term piece.
Pending UFA who started at linebacker for Washington the past two seasons. His departure opened the LB door, which was then addressed via the Leo Chenal signing.

signings.analysis: Marquee signing of the offseason for Washington. 4-year, $100M deal with $68M guaranteed at age 27 is a maximum-commitment investment. Oweh recorded 17.5 sacks over the past two seasons combined and provides the youth, length (6-5), and athleticism the Commanders desperately needed. Paired with Dorance Armstrong (returning from ACL tear) and Chaisson/Omenihu depth, EDGE goes from a critical tier-1 need to a fully addressed position. Drops 2 tiers minimum.

One-year prove-it deal with $10.3M guaranteed — structurally a high-floor bridge. Chaisson had a breakout year with New England (7.5 sacks, 10.5 in the postseason). At $11M AAV this is starter-level money on a one-year window. Adds genuine rotational value alongside Oweh but the 1-year structure signals they're not fully committing long-term to him. Complements the Oweh signing well. Need is already addressed by Oweh; Chaisson provides excellent depth insurance.

1-year depth signing at below-starter AAV. Omenihu provides power-based interior rush ability and a Super Bowl pedigree. He suffered a torn ACL in January 2024 and recorded only 1 sack in 6 games in 2025. High upside / high risk at a very cheap price. Pure depth behind Oweh and Chaisson. Does not move the EDGE need needle on its own but rounds out a now-loaded group.

Former Commanders DT returning to Washington on a 3-year deal. At $8M AAV this is mid-tier/starter-level money at IDL. Settle was best in 2024 (10 QB hits, 5 sacks) but had a poor 2025 with Houston (1 sack in 12 games). The Commanders' top-3 DTs combined for just 8 sacks and 20 QB hits in 2025. Settle adds a penetrator alongside existing pieces (Daron Payne, Javon Kinlaw). The 3-year structure shows investment but Settle needs to bounce back. IDL is partially addressed — still a potential draft target given the group's pass-rush deficiencies.

Excellent value signing. 3-year deal with $12.4M guaranteed at just 25 years old. Chenal is a two-time Super Bowl champion with Kansas City, known for his range, blitz ability, and versatility — key attributes for new DC Daronte Jones. At $8.25M AAV, this is below-market for his skill set. His cap hit in 2026 is only $4.32M. Replaces Bobby Wagner with a significantly younger, higher-upside player. LB is fully addressed and Washington has arguably upgraded the position.

A 2-year, $16M deal with $9.3M guaranteed is a legitimate starter-level investment at CB. Robertson is a versatile slot/outside corner who can align multiple ways under DC Daronte Jones. However, Washington entered free agency with only Trey Amos and Mike Sainristil guaranteed at CB, and Robertson is the only true external addition at the position. ESPN notes the team still needs to keep adding here. CB is partially addressed — Robertson shores up the slot but the Commanders still lack depth at outside CB and may target the position in the draft.

Strong signing. Cross, 24, established himself as a top-tier box safety over two seasons in Indianapolis, recording 266 tackles (second-most among all DBs over that span), 2.5 sacks, and 6 QB hits in 2025. The 2-year structure with ~$8M guaranteed at $7M AAV is solid starter money. He immediately upgrades a safety group that was overly inconsistent in 2025 (Will Harris injury, Quan Martin benched). S is effectively addressed with Cross, with Quan Martin and Harris still on roster.

Washington's first and most impactful offensive signing of free agency. 3-year deal at $10M AAV is solid mid-tier TE money ($10M sits right at the starter threshold). Okonkwo, 26, set career highs in 2025 (56 catches, 560 yards) and has never missed a game in 4 NFL seasons. He provides the 'move' TE the Commanders lacked — able to win outside, in the slot, and run after the catch. Directly replaces the departed Zach Ertz. Guarantees TBD exactly but $30M max over 3 years signals ~$18M effective guarantee estimate. TE need is fully addressed given the investment and player profile.

Bargain 1-year deal at $2M base ($4M with incentives). White had 1,539 yards from scrimmage in 2023 but his role diminished with Tampa Bay's emergence of Bucky Irving. His 205 career catches make him a strong pass-game complement to Jacory Croskey-Merritt, who needs development in that area. ESPN notes Washington still needs more at RB. One-year structure signals a bridge/depth piece, not the answer. RB remains a real need for the draft.

1-year reunion deal at $3M (down from his $10M deal with Jacksonville, which went poorly). Brown had a connection with Jayden Daniels in the playoffs two seasons ago as a Commander. A field-stretching option who fits OC Blough's desire for explosive plays. Not a starter-caliber WR2 solution; more of a depth/competition piece. WR remains a critical unaddressed need.

1-year minimum-adjacent deal. Jefferson averages nearly 12 yards per catch in his career but has been used in limited roles recently. Veteran depth addition, not a starter. WR remains Washington's most critical unaddressed need. Together Brown and Jefferson serve as floor-coverage insurance while the team awaits the draft or a potential Aiyuk/Diggs signing.

Re-signed former Titans first-round pick on a 1-year deal worth up to $4M. Burks has 63 career receptions over 35 games — has never broken through as a starter. Depth/reclamation project with upside given youth. Does not address the WR2 need alongside Terry McLaurin.

Franchise-defining extension. At $30.1M AAV with $61.5M in guarantees (including a $32.5M signing bonus — largest ever for an OL), Tunsil becomes the highest-paid offensive lineman in NFL history. He earned an 88.9 PFF pass block grade in 2025, ranking 2nd among all OL. This locks up Jayden Daniels' blindside protector through 2028. LT is permanently off the needs board.

2-year extension with upside to $10.5M. Wylie has started 34 games across 3 seasons in Washington and is a reliable veteran versatile OL. At $3.75M AAV this is depth/swing tackle pricing. With Biadasz released, Wylie slides into reserve OT/G depth but doesn't solve the center hole. IOL remains a real need at center.

Signed a new 2-year deal after Biadasz was released. Allegretti stepped in at center for Week 18 when Biadasz was injured and performed well. He's now the frontrunner to start at center, though he's a G/C by trade. At $4M AAV this is below-starter money at IOL, reflecting the developmental nature of the role. IOL need is partially addressed — Allegretti gives them a credible stopgap at center, but they still likely need to draft or sign a true starting-caliber center.

Re-signed as Jayden Daniels' backup. 1-year deal up to $11M with incentives. Serviceable veteran backup who knows the system. QB depth is addressed at a reasonable price. No impact on draft needs — Daniels is the unquestioned starter.

Re-signed for depth behind Oweh and Chaisson. Jackson is a former 49ers 2nd-rounder and a still-young developmental piece. Low-cost, low-risk retention. Adds depth to an already well-stocked EDGE group.
Re-signed after missing most of 2025 with a quad injury. Veteran depth/rotational EDGE. Terms not fully disclosed but fits depth pricing pattern. Round-out depth behind the premium signings.

Re-signed after being claimed off waivers from Tennessee during 2025. Depth DT with limited production (1 TFL, 11 tackles in 10 games in 2025). Fills out the IDL room behind Payne, Kinlaw, and Settle.
Re-signed veteran OL who appeared in 8 games with 3 starts in 2025. Swing tackle/depth piece. Does not address any structural need.
Re-signed 3x Pro Bowler and NFL's best punter. Led the NFL in percentage of punts downed inside the 20 (52.8%) in 2025. Retaining Way keeps elite special teams in place.
Re-signed after not being tendered as an RFA. Former 49ers 3rd-round pick with solid career (78% career FG). Kicker continuity maintained at low cost.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Oweh (4yr/$100M, $68M gtd) is a maximum commitment at age 27 — this is their long-term starter. Chaisson (1yr/$11M) adds legitimate rotational depth off career-best season. Omenihu, Drake Jackson, Deatrich Wise, and a returning Dorance Armstrong (5.5 sacks in 6 games pre-ACL) all add depth. Washington went from the worst EDGE group in the NFC to one of the deepest. Day 3 developmental pick only.

Leo Chenal (3yr/$24.75M, $12.4M gtd) at age 25 is a real investment in the position's future. He replaces Wagner with a younger, faster, higher-upside player. Frankie Luvu and Jordan Magee provide depth. LB is now a low-priority position for the draft.

Nick Cross (2yr/$14M) at age 24 is a legitimate starter-level investment. He immediately addresses the inconsistent safety play that plagued Washington in 2025. Will Harris and Quan Martin provide additional depth. S is off the draft board as a priority.

Chig Okonkwo (3yr/$30M) at age 26 replaces the aging/injured Zach Ertz with a younger, healthier move TE who has never missed a game. He provides the receiving weapon the Commanders' TE room lacked. TE is no longer a draft priority.

Laremy Tunsil extension (2yr/$60.2M, $61.5M gtd, $30.1M AAV) makes him the highest-paid OL in NFL history through 2028. LT is locked in long-term. Josh Conerly Jr. is the developing RT. Andrew Wylie provides veteran depth. OT is completely off the board.

Amik Robertson (2yr/$16M, $9.3M gtd) is a legitimate starter-level investment who can play slot or outside. However, Washington entered free agency with only Amos and Sainristil guaranteed at CB, and Robertson is the only true external addition. They lost Jonathan Jones to the Eagles and released Lattimore. CB remains a tier-2 need — still a strong draft target in rounds 1-3, possibly with the 7th overall pick.

Tim Settle (3yr/$24M) adds a penetrator who can complement Payne and Kinlaw. However, Settle's 2025 (1 sack in 12 games) is a major regression concern and the group as a whole produced only 8 sacks in 2025. IDL is partially addressed — still a potential mid-round draft target, especially if Daron Payne is traded (final year of contract).

Releasing Biadasz (signed by Chargers for 3yr/$30M) CREATED a starter-level center hole. Re-signing Allegretti (2yr) gives them a bridge option who auditioned well in Week 18, but Allegretti at $4M AAV is depth money. The Commanders need either a free agent center (they were interested in Linderbaum, who went to the Raiders) or a center in the draft. IOL remains a tier-2 need — guard depth is acceptable with Cosmi retained and Wylie re-signed, but center remains genuinely unsettled.

The Commanders LOST Deebo Samuel to free agency and STRUCK OUT on WR2 targets (Alec Pierce re-signed with Colts, Romeo Doubs went to Patriots). Dyami Brown (1yr/$3M) and Van Jefferson (1yr/$2M) are depth/competition pieces only. Treylon Burks (1yr/$4M) is a reclamation project. Terry McLaurin is the only true WR1 on the roster and reportedly entering the final year of his deal. WR is still an urgent tier-1 need. A WR with the 7th overall pick or a major signing (Brandon Aiyuk rumored) is likely the next big move.

Rachaad White (1yr/$2M) is a useful pass-catching back but not a featured runner and the structure is purely depth pricing. Jacory Croskey-Merritt is the only other RB under contract. The RB need was NOT resolved in free agency — they need either a mid-round draft pick or a second free agent signing to address this adequately.

key_additions.headline: Elite pass rusher (17.5 sacks over 2 seasons) fills the biggest defensive need on a maximum 4-year commitment
Record-breaking extension ($30.1M AAV, highest-paid OL ever) locks in Jayden Daniels' blindside protector through 2028
25-year-old two-time Super Bowl champion replaces Bobby Wagner at linebacker with youth and elite blitzing ability
Versatile slot/outside CB adds much-needed depth to a thin cornerback room that lost both Lattimore and Jonathan Jones
24-year-old box safety upgrades a position of need after ranking 2nd among all DBs in tackles over the past two seasons
First offensive weapon signed — 26-year-old move TE who set career highs (56 rec, 560 yds) replaces Zach Ertz for Jayden Daniels
Breakout pass rusher (7.5 sacks in 2025) adds high-quality depth behind Oweh on a one-year prove-it deal
Former Commander returns on multi-year deal to add interior pass-rush penetration to a DL group that was deficient in 2025

key_losses.headline: Starting center released to save cap space, leaving a genuine void with only Nick Allegretti as the internal replacement option
Four-time Pro Bowl CB released after torn ACL, deepening an already thin cornerback room entering the draft
2025 WR1 acquisition departs after underwhelming season, leaving Terry McLaurin as the only proven receiver on the roster
Career-high 5.5-sack edge rusher departs, but impact is mitigated by Washington's aggressive EDGE spending led by Oweh
Veteran CB who started 7 games in 2025 departs to division rival, further thinning the secondary depth
Two-year starting LB departs as a UFA but is adequately replaced by younger, cheaper Leo Chenal from Kansas City
